items.title: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India 2026 Best Moments
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>This is the energy, the people, and the community that makes KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India worth attending. From technical sessions to community gatherings, the cloud native ecosystem showed up in full force.

#KubeCon #CloudNative #CNCF</article>

items.title: ChatLoopBackOff: Episode 78 (Cedar)
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>In this episode of ChatLoopBackOff, CNCF Ambassador Henrik Rexed explores Cedar for the first time. Cedar is an open-source authorization policy language designed to decouple access control from application logic.

Core Capabilities:
* Decoupled Security: Separates authorization logic from code, enabling updates without redeployment.
* Flexible Models: Supports Role-Based (RBAC), Attribute-Based (ABAC), and Relationship-Based (ReBAC) access control models.
* Mathematical Rigor: Formally verified language specification using theorem provers to ensure precise evaluation.
High Performance: Designed for real-time evaluation with bounded latency in cloud-native workloads.
* Henrik will provide a hands-on look at how Cedar functions, offering an honest, first-time assessment of its syntax and tools.

Join us on June 25, 2026, for this live dive into Cedar!</article>

items.title: Swapping the Engine Mid-Flight: How We Moved Reddit’s Petabyte Scale Kafka Fleet to ... | S. Kistler
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Swapping the Engine Mid-Flight: How We Moved Reddit’s Petabyte Scale Kafka Fleet to Kubernetes - Sky Kistler, Reddit

Migrating a petabyte-scale Kafka fleet is not a simple lift and shift. In this session, Sky Kistler shares how Reddit moved approximately 500 Kafka brokers from EC2 to Strimzi on Kubernetes without scheduled downtime or data loss.

The talk walks through the constraints that shaped the migration, including zero downtime, no mass client coordination, and reversibility at every stage. Sky explains how Reddit built a hybrid EC2 and Strimzi cluster, used DNS and listener configuration to support the transition, and relied on Cruise Control to move replicas and leadership safely.

Attendees will learn key lessons from the migration, including what to monitor, why MirrorMaker 2 was not the right fit for Reddit’s requirements, how the team completed the move from ZooKeeper to KRaft, and the operational improvements Reddit saw after moving to Strimzi.</article>

items.title: Building Strimzi MCP Server for Kubernetes: Democratizing Platform Exper... | D. Kornel, J. Stejskal
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Building Strimzi MCP Server for Kubernetes: Democratizing Platform Expertise Through LLMs - David Kornel & Jakub Stejskal, IBM

Managing Kubernetes and Strimzi clusters often requires deep platform knowledge, multiple tools, and manual troubleshooting across commands, logs, metrics, and dashboards. This session introduces the Strimzi Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, an open source tool designed to make that expertise more accessible through LLM-powered workflows.

David Kornel and Jakub Stejskal explain how the Strimzi MCP server connects AI agents to Kubernetes clusters through a read-only interface, allowing users to ask natural language questions while safely retrieving structured data, logs, and metrics.

Attendees will learn how the MCP server supports diagnostics, prompt templates, incident response, local or in-cluster deployment, and future improvements such as Kubernetes impersonation, Kafka Admin API integration, and expanded community contributions.</article>

items.title: Keynote | Kate Stanley and Paolo Patierno, IBM
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

In this StrimziCon 2026 opening keynote, Kate Stanley and Paolo Patierno welcome the community and highlight key updates from the past year, including the Strimzi 1.0 release, the transition to the V1 CRD API, upcoming Gateway API support, cert-manager integration, auto-rebalancing with Cruise Control, and the project’s continued progress toward CNCF graduation.</article>

items.title: From Running to Operating: Real-World Strimzi in Production | Rajith Attapattu, Randoli
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

From Running to Operating: Real-World Strimzi in Production - Rajith Attapattu, Randoli

Running Strimzi in production means moving beyond deployment and into day-to-day operations. In this session, Rajith Attapattu shares lessons from running Strimzi and Apache Kafka for an observability platform, including how data classification shapes topic configuration, replication, acknowledgments, partitioning, retention, and cleanup policies.

The talk covers monitoring with OpenTelemetry, key Kafka and broker metrics, consumer lag troubleshooting, and how Kubernetes metadata can help identify the specific pods behind production issues.

Attendees will learn practical strategies for safer upgrades, parallel cluster migrations, high availability with topology spread constraints, and balancing reliability with operational cost.</article>

items.title: Closing | Kate Stanley and Paolo Patierno, IBM
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Closing - Kate Stanley & Paolo Patierno, IBM

Kate Stanley and Paolo Patierno close out StrimziCon 2026 with reflections from the event, appreciation for the speakers and CNCF, and a look at the community feedback shared throughout the conference.

The session highlights favorite Strimzi features such as Kafka Connect integration, MirrorMaker, rolling updates, ZooKeeper-to-KRaft migration, node pools, and Cruise Control, along with future areas of development including cert-manager integration, Kafka roller updates, and new ways to customize reconciliation.

Attendees are encouraged to continue supporting Strimzi by contributing to the project, upvoting issues, trying new features, sharing the project with others, and staying connected through Slack, community calls, social media, and YouTube.</article>

items.title: Disaster Recovery in Action with Kafka and Strimzi | Mickael Maison and Gantigmaa Selenge, IBM
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Disaster Recovery in Action with Kafka and Strimzi - Mickael Maison & Gantigmaa Selenge, IBM

Disaster recovery is essential for keeping Kafka workloads available when failures happen, from full region outages to accidental topic deletion. This session explains key DR concepts in Apache Kafka and Strimzi, including primary and target clusters, failover, failback, and how MirrorMaker supports recovery workflows.

Mickael Maison and Gantigmaa Selenge walk through the MirrorMaker source, checkpoint, and heartbeat connectors, explaining what they do, where they should run, and the limitations operators need to understand around topic deletions, ACLs, and offset translation.

Attendees will learn how to choose a mirroring starting point, monitor replication health, and plan both scheduled and unplanned failover processes using Kafka and Strimzi.</article>

items.title: Who Are You? Configuring Kafka Authentication in Strimzi from TLS to Custom Principals | D. Mulder
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Who Are You? Configuring Kafka Authentication in Strimzi from TLS to Custom Principals - Daniel Mulder, Axual

Authentication in multi-tenant Kafka environments can quickly become complex. This session follows Axual’s journey from simple, single-tenant Kafka clusters to multi-tenant Strimzi deployments with different PKI and Certificate Authority requirements.

Daniel Mulder explains how mTLS works in Strimzi, how certificate identities are mapped to Kafka principals, and how custom Principal Builders can help prevent authorization conflicts and privilege escalation. The session also covers the operational tradeoffs of managing multiple CAs, custom images, and Strimzi version updates.

Attendees will learn options for advanced Kafka authentication in Strimzi, including custom listener configurations, OAuth/OIDC-based authentication, username prefixes, and layered setups that combine mTLS with OAuth for stronger tenant isolation.</article>

items.title: Beyond the Install: Take Full Ownership of Your Strimzi Cluster | Jakub Scholz, Cloudera
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Mumbai, India (18-19 June, 2026), Yokohama, Japan (29-30 July, 2026), and Shanghai, China (8-9 September, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Beyond the Install: Take Full Ownership of Your Strimzi Cluster - Jakub Scholz, Cloudera

Running Strimzi in production does not end after installation. This session explores the shared responsibility model between the Strimzi project and its users, outlining what the maintainers provide and what users need to own when operating secure, reliable clusters.

Jakub Scholz explains how the project approaches releases, community support, patch updates, Kafka version support, CVE handling, and container image updates. He also highlights why staying up to date is important for receiving fixes and making community support easier.

Attendees will learn practical ways to take ownership of their Strimzi installations, including mirroring images and installation files, testing changes, building from source when needed, and contributing back to help keep the project stable and sustainable.</article>

items.title: CNCF On-Demand: Building a fully open source private cloud with Kubernetes & OpenStack
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>As more organizations look to move away from vendor lock-in, maintain data sovereignty, and achieve cost predictability, private cloud is making a strong comeback and it can be built entirely with open-source tools.

In this session, Guilherme Steinmüller from VEXXHOST walks through how to build and operate a production-grade private cloud using a 100% open-source stack including OpenStack, Kubernetes, Ceph, Prometheus, and Grafana — all deployed and managed using Atmosphere, an open-source OpenStack distribution.

You'll see a live demo of spinning up Kubernetes clusters on OpenStack using Magnum, with Cluster API managing the lifecycle under the hood. We'll also cover Day 2 operations – monitoring, upgrades, and scaling, and share key lessons learned from running this in production.

Whether you're evaluating private cloud options or already operating one, this session will give you a practical, real-world look at what a fully open-source infrastructure stack looks like from bare metal to running workloads.</article>

items.title: CNCF On-Demand: Zero-Downtime Migration - Ingress NGINX to Envoy Gateway
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Ingress NGINX entered end-of-life in March 2026, leaving teams running it in production without a clear migration path. This session covers a real production migration from Ingress NGINX to Envoy Gateway in the cloud, featuring zero dropped requests, no application team involvement, and a complete cutover verified through continuous polling.

Andrew and Joachim walk through the full process: evaluating five alternative controllers against production requirements, validating in internal environments before touching the customer cluster, running both controllers in parallel on live infrastructure using weighted DNS, and executing the final cutover by shifting Route 53 weights rather than swapping DNS records.

Topics covered include annotation-to-HTTPRoute mapping, mTLS compatibility across controller options, parallel operation with Goldilocks, ExternalDNS integration for weighted routing, cert-manager behavior under Gateway API, and why weighted DNS eliminates the TTL risk that makes traditional cutovers dangerous.

Attendees leave with a repeatable playbook for zero-downtime Ingress migrations, a clear picture of where Envoy Gateway fits in the Kubernetes networking landscape today, and the specific patterns that make incremental traffic shifting safer than any hard cutover.</article>

items.title: What makes CNCF different from other foundations?
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>What makes CNCF different from other foundations? It's practitioner-led.

In this clip, Buu Lam of F5 shares why having hands-on practitioners drive project direction gives CNCF a unique advantage: a clear, real-world understanding of what the market needs.

#CNCF #CloudNative #Kubernetes</article>

items.title: CNCF On-Demand: Introduction to OpenTelemetry Kotlin
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>In this session, Jamie Lynch (senior software engineer at Embrace and a maintainer in the OpenTelemetry Kotlin SIG) introduces OpenTelemetry Kotlin, a Kotlin Multiplatform implementation of the OpenTelemetry specification. With it, developers can now capture telemetry on various platforms, including JVM, Android, iOS, and JavaScript, using a consistent Kotlin API. The new Kotlin SDK has two modes of operation: compatibility mode, which wraps OpenTelemetry Java, and implementation mode, which uses Kotlin Multiplatform.

Jamie also covers a step-by-step guide on setting up the Kotlin SDK, instrumenting an application with traces and logs, and exporting telemetry data.

The OpenTelemetry Kotlin project is new, and Jamie encourages the CNCF community to contribute, whether through code, feedback, or documentation, to stabilize and enhance the SDK. He shares the priorities for the Kotlin SIG, including stabilizing the logging and tracing APIs and developing instrumentation for widely-used libraries like Ktor.

You can help the OpenTelemetry Kotlin project by:
* Trying the project in your app and giving feedback
* Joining the #otel-kotlin channel in the CNCF slack
* Giving feedback at https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-kotlin/issues
* Writing code or documentation that helps progress the implementation</article>
